title: Polymer clay textured keychain
subtitle: A small textured shape with a baked-in eyelet for a keyring
excerpt: A flat textured polymer-clay shape baked rigid with a wire eyelet pressed into the back. The eyelet forms the attachment point for a keyring. The piece is light, hard-wearing, and small enough to bake in one batch on a single tray.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay keychain construction derived from first principles. Domestic-oven curing schedule follows the manufacturer-published curing range (110-130°C for 15-30 minutes) standard across the major polymer clay families.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that sets in a domestic oven at 110-130°C. Decorative use only, not food-safe.
The heat process that sets polymer clay in a domestic oven, fusing the PVC particles into a hard plastic.
A small loop of wire pressed into the back of the clay before curing, which becomes the attachment point for a keyring or jump ring.

content.text: A flat textured shape pressed from
polymer clay

, with a small wire loop pressed into the back before baking. Once cured, a keyring or jump ring slips through the loop and the piece is ready to use. No kiln is required: polymer clay sets in a domestic oven at 110-130°C in 20-30 minutes.

attrs.title: Baking polymer clay
attrs.body: Polymer clay is not food-safe. Use a dedicated baking tray and a piece of greaseproof paper kept for clay work. Verify the oven temperature with a thermometer the first time, as a too-hot oven scorches polymer clay above 150°C.

items.name: Eye pin or 1 mm steel wire, 30 mm length
items.substitution: Bent paperclip works for a single piece

items.name: Cookie cutter or fettling knife for shape

content.text: Conditioning the clay

A fresh block of polymer clay is stiff straight from the packet. Warm it in the hands and work it for two or three minutes, pressing and folding until it softens into a pliable mass. Conditioning evenly distributes the plasticiser through the block; under-conditioned clay cracks at the edges when rolled.

A well-conditioned block bends in half without any surface cracks along the fold. If small cracks open along the bend, keep working the clay until they stop appearing. Cold weather makes the block stiffer; warming the packet in a pocket or on a sunny windowsill for ten minutes before unwrapping helps.
Choosing the texture source

The texture is the main visual feature of the keychain. A piece of cotton lace records every thread crisply. A small fresh leaf (sage, mint, ivy) gives a botanical print with the leaf veins standing out. A pressed dried flower works similarly. A coarse linen or hessian fabric gives a woven texture. Rubber stamps cut for clay work record their pattern cleanly because they are designed for the soft material. Avoid open-weave plastic mesh: it can grip the clay and tear when peeled.
Making the keychain
1. Roll a slab

Roll the conditioned clay between two slats or guides to an even 4 mm thickness. Aim for a slab about 60 mm square. The 4 mm thickness gives a finished piece sturdy enough to take daily handling without snapping at thin spots.

Work on a smooth tile, a sheet of greaseproof paper, or a glass surface. Polymer clay picks up texture from anything textured underneath it, including the wood grain of a board. The slab should lift cleanly off the work surface without tearing; if it sticks, run a thin metal spatula under it to free the edge.
2. Press the texture

Lay the texture source on top of the slab. A piece of lace, a pressed leaf, a rubber texture stamp, or a coarse fabric all work. Roll over once with the rolling pin using firm even pressure. Peel the texture away gently; the impression should be crisp without dragging.
3. Cut the shape

With a small cookie cutter or the fettling knife, cut the keychain shape from the textured slab. A round 35 mm disc, an oval 40 by 28 mm, or a softly rectangular 45 by 25 mm tile all work. Smooth any rough edges with a damp fingertip.

Keep the shape solid. Cut-out details (a heart with a hole in the middle, a leaf with a fretted edge) work in polymer clay, but they add stress points where the piece can crack with handling. For a first project, a full unbroken shape with the texture as the only decoration is the most reliable result.
4. Form and embed the eyelet
Bend the wire into a small loop, leaving two 10 mm straight legs sticking out from the loop's base. The loop sits about 6 mm above the legs, with the legs splayed apart by 5 mm to lock into the clay.

Use needle-nose pliers to make a clean round loop. A loop bent by hand around a pencil tends to be slightly uneven and can stick out at an angle once embedded, making the keychain hang crooked. Plier-bent loops sit straight every time.

Test-fit the loop in the clay before pressing it in fully. If the loop tilts in one direction, adjust the angle of the legs before embedding. Once the clay closes around the wire, the angle is set and the loop is hard to straighten without redoing the back of the piece.
Press the legs of the
eyelet loop

into the back of the shape near the top edge, sliding them in horizontally so they lie hidden inside the clay. The loop itself stands proud of the clay surface by 4-5 mm, enough to take a keyring. Smooth the back of the clay over the embedded legs with a fingertip so no wire shows from behind.
5. Bake
Lay the keychain texture-side up on a greaseproof-paper-lined baking tray. Bake at 110°C (fan) for 25 minutes.
Curing
at this temperature for the full time hardens the clay throughout without scorching the surface. Under-baked polymer clay is brittle and snaps at the eyelet; the full 25 minutes is the difference.

Let the piece cool on the tray for 15 minutes before handling. Polymer clay reaches full strength as it cools, not while it is still warm. Slip a split-ring keyring through the loop and the keychain is finished.
Finishing

The baked piece can be left bare or painted with acrylic into the recessed texture lines, then wiped with a soft cloth to leave colour only in the depressions. The contrast between the painted recesses and the cleaned high points pulls the texture forward. A coat of acrylic varnish protects painted finishes and gives a soft sheen across the surface.

For metallic effects, mica powder rubbed across the high points before baking sticks to the surface during curing and stays in place. Gold, copper, and pewter mica all read well on a darker base colour. Apply with a fingertip in small amounts; excess powder brushes off the recesses with a soft brush before baking.

Sand the edges with 320-grit sandpaper if the cut left rough spots, then 600-grit for a smooth feel. Wet sanding (with the sandpaper held under a running tap or dipped in water) keeps the polymer dust down and prevents the sandpaper clogging.
Care

The keychain is hard-wearing and weather-resistant. Avoid prolonged direct sunlight at high temperatures, which can soften polymer clay over months. Not for the dishwasher; wipe clean with a damp cloth. If the wire eyelet starts to wiggle in the clay after long use, a drop of cyanoacrylate (superglue) inside the loop where it meets the clay re-anchors it.

Polymer clay does not corrode, fade significantly indoors, or absorb moisture. A keychain in daily pocket use can be expected to keep its colour and texture for years. The split-ring keyring will rust before the polymer clay shows wear.
Variations
Roll two thin slabs of contrasting clay colours together before pressing the texture for a marbled background. Twisting the two slabs once before rolling deepens the marble effect.

Use a fresh leaf as the texture source for a botanical print: ferns, sage, and oak leaves all give a strong impression at this scale. A pressed flower works too, though delicate petals sometimes stick to the clay and need lifting carefully with a needle.
Press a small handmade stamp of initials or a date into the back for a personalised gift piece. A letter set carved from a wine cork works for a one-off.
Bake several keychains at once to fill the tray. Polymer clay does not change colour when curing as long as the temperature is correct, so a tray of mixed colours bakes together without bleeding.
Add a second small loop at the bottom of the keychain shape before baking for a charm or tassel to hang from.

items.cause: Wire legs were too short or too straight, or pressed in only a few millimetres
items.fix: Bend the legs apart at a 30-degree splay before embedding, and push them at least 10 mm into the clay so they lock

items.cause: Oven temperature too high or hot spot above the tray
items.fix: Verify temperature with an oven thermometer; cover loosely with a foil tent if the oven runs hot at the top

items.symptom: Keychain snaps at the loop in use
items.cause: Under-baked, or shape too thin at the loop attachment
items.fix: Bake for the full 25 minutes at the correct temperature; aim for at least 4 mm thickness across the eyelet area

items.cause: Rolling pin moved sideways during pressing, or the texture source was too flexible
items.fix: Press straight down without sliding; for fabric or lace, lay it flat on a hard surface first and press the clay onto it instead of rolling over
